title: Sustainable development
summary: Growth raises output today, but at what cost to tomorrow? This lesson builds the idea of sustainable development — meeting present needs without compromising future generations — and separates it cleanly from economic growth and economic development. It works through the tensions between the three, the main threats to sustainability (over-use of common access resources, pollution, climate change and resource depletion), and the two policy families examiners expect you to weigh: market-based tools (carbon taxes, tradable permits, funding clean technology) and interventionist ones (regulation, international agreements).
learningObjectives: Define sustainable development and explain the link between poverty, growth and the environment.
Distinguish economic growth, economic development and environmental sustainability, and explain the tensions between them.
Explain the main threats to sustainability: over-use of common access resources, pollution, climate change and resource depletion.
Evaluate market-based approaches (carbon taxes, tradable permits, funding clean technology) and interventionist approaches (regulation, international agreements).
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Don't Spend the Future's Resources
simpleExplanation.summary: Sustainable development means improving living standards now without using up the resources or wrecking the environment that future generations will need. It is a bigger idea than just 'growth' and it is not the same as 'protecting nature' — it tries to do both at once, over the long run.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of the planet's resources as a shared savings account that every generation draws on. If you only spend the interest — using resources at a rate that lets them regenerate — the account stays healthy forever. If you start spending the capital itself — cutting forests faster than they regrow, emitting CO2 faster than the atmosphere can absorb it — you get richer today but leave a smaller account for whoever comes next. Sustainable development is the discipline of living off the interest.
steps: Start with the definition: development that meets present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
Separate three ideas that students constantly confuse: economic GROWTH (more output), economic DEVELOPMENT (better living standards), and SUSTAINABILITY (can it last?).
See the tension: the fastest way to grow output is often the least sustainable, and the poorest people are frequently pushed into environmentally damaging choices.
Weigh the fixes: market-based tools (carbon taxes, tradable permits, funding clean tech) and interventionist tools (regulation, international agreements) — each with strengths and limits.

flashcards.back: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. The key idea is inter-generational equity.

Growth is an increase in real output (real GDP). Development is a broad improvement in living standards — incomes, health, education, freedoms. Growth can occur without development, and neither is automatically sustainable.
Using natural resources and ecosystems at a rate that does not degrade them for the future — living off nature's 'interest', not its 'capital'.

Resources that are non-excludable (no one can be stopped from using them) but rivalrous (one person's use reduces what is left) — oceans, fish stocks, the atmosphere, forests. Because no one owns them, no one has a private incentive to conserve them.

When a common access resource is over-used because each user gains the full private benefit of using more while the cost of depletion is shared by everyone. The result is over-consumption and eventual collapse of the resource.

Poverty can force short-term survival choices (deforestation for fuel, over-farming) that damage the environment; but the growth that lifts people out of poverty can itself be highly polluting. Sustainable development tries to break this trade-off.
A market-based tax per tonne of CO2 (or other emissions). It internalises the negative externality by making polluters pay, giving firms and consumers a continuous incentive to cut emissions.

A cap-and-trade scheme: government sets a total emissions cap, issues permits up to that cap, and lets firms buy and sell them. The cap guarantees the environmental outcome; trading directs cuts to where they are cheapest.

Subsidies, grants or public R&D spending on low-carbon technology (renewables, batteries, carbon capture). It lowers the cost of the sustainable option rather than raising the cost of the polluting one.

Climate change is a global common access problem, so countries have an incentive to free-ride on others' cuts. Agreements like the Paris framework rely on self-set targets with weak enforcement, so promises can exceed action.
Wrong. Sustainability asks whether development can LAST. It has environmental, economic and social dimensions — a policy can be green yet economically or socially unsustainable, and vice versa.
These are three distinct concepts. More output (growth) is not the same as better lives (development), and neither guarantees that the improvement can be maintained over time (sustainability).

quickCheck.prompt: A country's real GDP rises 6% in a year, driven entirely by burning newly discovered coal reserves with no pollution controls. This is best described as:
options: Sustainable development
Economic growth that may not be sustainable
Economic development but not growth
Environmental sustainability
quickCheck.answer: Economic growth that may not be sustainable

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement about sustainability is CORRECT?
options: Sustainability is only concerned with protecting the natural environment
A policy that is environmentally clean is automatically sustainable
Sustainability asks whether improvements in living standards can be maintained for future generations
Sustainability and economic growth always move together
quickCheck.answer: Sustainability asks whether improvements in living standards can be maintained for future generations

quickCheck.prompt: Ocean fish stocks are over-exploited mainly because they are:
options: Excludable and non-rivalrous
Non-excludable and rivalrous common access resources
Owned by a single firm that overproduces
Free goods with no opportunity cost
quickCheck.answer: Non-excludable and rivalrous common access resources

quickCheck.prompt: The key difference between a carbon TAX and a system of tradable PERMITS is that:
options: A tax fixes the quantity of emissions; permits fix the price
A tax fixes the price of emitting; permits fix the total quantity of emissions
Only permits raise government revenue
Only a tax is a market-based policy
quickCheck.answer: A tax fixes the price of emitting; permits fix the total quantity of emissions

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes: 'Economic development means the same as economic growth.' The best correction is:
options: They are identical, so the student is right
Growth is about rising real output; development is a broader rise in living standards such as health and education
Development is only about the environment
Growth always causes development one-for-one
quickCheck.answer: Growth is about rising real output; development is a broader rise in living standards such as health and education

faq.q: Is sustainable development the same as being anti-growth?
faq.a: No. It is against growth that depletes natural or social capital faster than it can be replaced. Sustainable development supports growth that can continue over the long run — for example, growth built on renewable energy, efficient resource use and rising human capital. The objection is to the KIND of growth, not to growth itself.

faq.q: How do I keep growth, development and sustainability straight in an essay?
faq.a: Use one clean sentence for each. Growth = an increase in real GDP (more output). Development = a broad improvement in living standards, including health, education and freedoms (better lives). Sustainability = whether those improvements can be maintained for future generations (can it last?). Examiners reward candidates who define all three separately before discussing how they interact.

faq.q: Why can't the market solve environmental problems on its own?
faq.a: Because the environment is largely a common access resource. Clean air and stable climate are non-excludable, so no firm can charge for them and no one has a private incentive to protect them. The social cost of pollution is not reflected in market prices, so the market over-produces the polluting good. This market failure is the reason government intervention (taxes, permits, regulation) is needed.

faq.q: Which is better: a carbon tax or tradable permits?
faq.a: It depends on what you most want to control. A carbon tax fixes the price of polluting but leaves the total quantity of emissions uncertain. Tradable permits fix the total quantity (through the cap) but let the price vary. If certainty about the environmental outcome matters most, permits are attractive; if certainty about costs to firms matters most, a tax is attractive. Both are market-based and both internalise the externality.

sections.content: For most of modern history the headline goal of economic policy was simply more output — a higher GDP. But the by-products of that pursuit, from air pollution to a changing climate to exhausted fisheries, have forced a rethink. This lesson introduces sustainable development: the idea that we should raise living standards today in a way that does not rob future generations of the resources and environment they will need. Crucially, we will keep three ideas apart — economic growth, economic development and environmental sustainability — because confusing them is one of the most common ways students lose marks on this topic.
What sustainable development means

Sustainable development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. The central principle is inter-generational equity: today's economic activity should not use up essential resources, or cause irreversible environmental damage, in ways that leave people in the future worse off. A useful mental model is to treat the environment as a stock of natural capital. If we consume its output (fish caught, timber grown, CO2 absorbed) no faster than it regenerates, the stock survives; if we draw the stock down, we get richer now and poorer later.

This is where poverty enters the story. The link between poverty, growth and the environment runs in both directions. Poverty can push people into environmentally damaging survival choices — clearing forests for firewood, farming marginal land until it degrades — because the long-term future is a luxury the very poor cannot afford to prioritise. At the same time, the rapid growth that lifts populations out of poverty has historically been resource-hungry and polluting. Sustainable development is the attempt to break this trade-off: to raise the living standards of the poor without destroying the natural base that everyone, present and future, depends on.
Growth, development and sustainability are three different things

Examiners see these three terms treated as synonyms far too often. They are not. Getting the distinctions right is the single biggest driver of marks on this topic, so pin them down before going further.

items: **Economic growth** — an increase in real output (real GDP) over time. It is a purely quantitative measure: are we producing more this year than last?

**Economic development** — a broad, qualitative improvement in living standards: higher real incomes, but also better health, education, life expectancy and greater freedoms. Growth can occur with little development (if the gains go to a narrow elite), and development can occur with modest growth (if a country invests well in health and schooling).

**Environmental sustainability** — whether the improvement can be maintained. It asks whether current production and consumption can continue without degrading the natural resources and ecosystems future generations will rely on.

sections.content: Now the tensions. In the short run, the cheapest way to grow output is often the least sustainable — burning fossil fuels, cutting old-growth forest, over-fishing. So growth and sustainability can pull against each other. Development and sustainability can also conflict: a poor country may reasonably prioritise raising incomes and health today over conserving resources for a future generation that will (it hopes) be richer. But the relationships are not always negative. Well-designed growth — powered by clean technology and efficient resource use — can raise output AND be sustainable, and rising development (education, better institutions) tends to make a society more willing and able to protect its environment. The examiner's reward goes to students who show BOTH the tensions and the ways the three can be reconciled.

When a question mentions any two of growth, development and sustainability, define all three separately in your first paragraph, then discuss how they interact. A script that treats them as one idea is capped in the lower bands no matter how much else it says.
Threats to sustainability

The obstacles to sustainable development share a common root: the natural environment is largely a **common access resource (CAR)** — non-excludable (no one can be prevented from using it) but rivalrous (one person's use reduces what remains). Because no one owns clean air, fish stocks or a stable climate, no one has a private incentive to conserve them, and market prices ignore the damage done. This is a classic negative-externality market failure, and it produces four linked threats.

items: **Over-use of common access resources** — the tragedy of the commons. Each fisher, logger or herder captures the full private gain from using more, while the cost of depletion is spread across everyone. The rational individual choice adds up to collective ruin: collapsed fisheries, deforested land, exhausted aquifers.

**Pollution** — production and consumption impose external costs (dirty air, contaminated water) that are not paid by the polluter. Output settles above the socially efficient level because the private cost is below the true social cost.

**Climate change** — the largest common access problem of all. Greenhouse gases from anywhere warm the atmosphere everywhere, so no single country bears the full cost of its own emissions, and every country has an incentive to let others cut first.

**Resource depletion** — non-renewable resources (oil, minerals) are finite, and renewable resources (forests, fish) become effectively non-renewable if harvested faster than they regenerate. Depletion transfers a cost onto future generations who inherit a smaller stock.

sections.question: A coastal region's fishery can sustainably yield 10,000 tonnes of fish per year — the rate at which the stock naturally replenishes. There are 100 independent boats. Each boat's owner reasons: 'If I catch an extra 20 tonnes, I earn the full revenue from it; the slight fall in next year's stock is shared across all 100 boats, so I personally lose almost nothing.' Explain, using the idea of common access resources, why this reasoning leads to over-fishing and threatens the fishery's sustainability.
sections.solution:
Fish stocks are a **common access resource**: non-excludable (any boat can fish the open sea) and rivalrous (a fish caught by one boat cannot be caught by another).

**1. Private incentive vs social cost.** Each owner receives 100% of the revenue from catching an extra 20 tonnes — that is the private benefit. But the cost, a smaller breeding stock and lower future yields, is shared across all 100 boats. So each owner privately bears only about 1/100th of the cost of their own extra catch while keeping all of the benefit.

**2. The dominant choice.** Because private benefit exceeds the privately-felt cost, every owner independently chooses to expand their catch. Since all 100 reason identically, total catch pushes well above the sustainable yield of 10,000 tonnes.

**3. The result — the tragedy of the commons.** Harvesting faster than the stock regenerates shrinks the breeding population, so next year's sustainable yield falls, prompting even harder fishing to maintain incomes. The stock can collapse entirely.

**Conclusion:** No individual is acting irrationally — each responds sensibly to the incentives they face. The failure is structural: because the resource is unowned and unpriced, the sum of rational private choices is collective over-use. This is precisely why sustainability of a common access resource usually requires intervention (quotas, tradable permits or enforceable property rights) rather than being left to the market.

sections.content: Approaches to sustainability: market-based tools

The economist's toolkit for sustainability splits into two families. The first, **market-based approaches**, work by changing prices and incentives so that the market itself steers towards a sustainable outcome — 'internalising the externality' so that polluters face the true social cost of their actions.

items: **Carbon taxes** — a tax per tonne of CO2 emitted. It raises the private cost of polluting towards the social cost, gives every firm and household a continuous incentive to cut emissions and switch to cleaner alternatives, and raises revenue that can fund green spending. Weakness: the resulting quantity of emissions is uncertain, and the tax can be regressive, hitting low-income households hardest.

**Tradable permits (cap-and-trade)** — the government sets a total emissions cap, issues permits up to that cap, and lets firms buy and sell them. The cap guarantees the environmental outcome, while trading channels emission cuts to the firms that can achieve them most cheaply. Weakness: setting the cap correctly is hard, permit prices can be volatile, and an over-generous cap achieves little.

**Funding clean technology** — subsidies, grants and public R&D that lower the cost of the sustainable option (renewables, batteries, carbon capture) rather than raising the cost of the dirty one. Weakness: it has an opportunity cost for the government budget, and picking which technologies to back is risky.

sections.question: A government wants electricity firms to cut total CO2 emissions from 100 million tonnes to 80 million tonnes per year. It is choosing between (a) a carbon tax of $40 per tonne and (b) issuing 80 million tradable permits (each allowing one tonne of CO2). Explain the key trade-off between the two policies, and identify one situation in which each would be preferred.
sections.solution:
Both are market-based and both put a price on carbon, but they fix different variables.

**1. What a carbon tax fixes.** The tax sets the PRICE of emitting ($40/tonne) but leaves the QUANTITY of emissions uncertain. Firms cut emissions up to the point where the cost of cutting one more tonne equals $40. If cutting turns out to be cheaper than expected, emissions could fall well below 80mt; if it is more expensive, they might stay above it. The government cannot be sure of hitting exactly 80mt, but firms know their cost per tonne.

**2. What tradable permits fix.** Issuing exactly 80 million permits sets the QUANTITY of emissions (80mt, guaranteed by the cap) but leaves the PRICE uncertain — the permit price is set by supply and demand in the permit market and could be above or below $40.

**3. The trade-off.** You can pin down price certainty (tax) OR quantity certainty (permits), but not both. 

**Preferred cases:**
- Choose **permits** when the environmental target is critical and must be met — e.g. staying under a scientifically-set emissions ceiling. Certainty of quantity matters most.
- Choose the **tax** when protecting firms from unpredictable costs matters most — e.g. to avoid a volatile permit price damaging competitiveness. Certainty of price matters most.

**Conclusion:** Neither is universally 'better'. The right choice depends on whether the policymaker most needs certainty about the environmental outcome or certainty about the cost to the economy.

sections.content: Approaches to sustainability: interventionist tools
The second family, **interventionist approaches**, does not rely on adjusting market prices. Instead the government (or a group of governments) sets rules directly.

items: **Regulation (command-and-control)** — legal limits and standards: emissions ceilings, bans on harmful substances (e.g. single-use plastics), mandated technology (catalytic converters), fishing quotas or protected areas. Strength: certain and easy to understand; a clear line firms cannot cross. Weakness: it gives no incentive to cut BELOW the limit, can be costly to monitor and enforce, and a uniform rule ignores that some firms can cut far more cheaply than others.

**International agreements** — because climate change, ocean pollution and biodiversity loss cross borders, no single country can solve them alone. Frameworks such as the Paris Agreement and the UN Sustainable Development Goals coordinate collective action and set shared targets. Strength: they tackle the global scale of the problem. Weakness: enforcement is weak — targets are often self-set with no real penalties, so countries have an incentive to free-ride on others' efforts, and promises frequently outrun delivery.

sections.content: In practice, sustainable development is rarely achieved by any single instrument. Real policy usually blends them: a carbon tax to price emissions, regulation to ban the worst practices outright, clean-tech funding to make the green alternative viable, and an international agreement to stop the effort being undercut abroad. Evaluation questions reward candidates who recognise this and who weigh each tool's effectiveness, cost and unintended effects rather than declaring one 'best'.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating growth, development and sustainability as the same thing** — growth is more output, development is better living standards, sustainability is whether the improvement can last. Blurring them is the fastest way to be capped in the lower bands.

**Claiming sustainability is 'only about the environment'** — sustainability also has economic and social dimensions. A policy can be environmentally clean yet unsustainable if it is unaffordable or socially unacceptable, and a green outcome is not automatically a sustainable one.

**Assuming growth is always bad for the environment** — the argument is about the KIND of growth. Clean-technology-based growth can raise output and be sustainable; blanket 'growth harms the planet' claims miss the marks for nuance.

**Confusing a carbon tax with tradable permits** — a tax fixes the price of emitting and leaves quantity uncertain; permits fix the total quantity and leave the price uncertain. Getting this the wrong way round is a classic error.

**Ignoring the free-rider problem in international agreements** — evaluation of global policy is thin unless you explain why countries have an incentive to let others cut first, which is why enforcement is the central weakness.

**Listing policies without evaluating them** — naming carbon taxes, permits, regulation and agreements earns little on its own. Marks come from weighing each one's effectiveness, cost and side-effects against the alternatives.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain the relationship between economic growth, economic development and environmental sustainability. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Economic growth, economic development and environmental sustainability are three distinct concepts, and the relationship between them is one of both tension and potential reinforcement.

*Economic growth* is an increase in an economy's real output, measured by a rise in real GDP. It is a purely quantitative idea: it tells us the economy is producing more, but nothing about how those gains are used or distributed. *Economic development* is broader and qualitative — a general improvement in living standards, including higher real incomes but also better health, education, life expectancy and greater economic freedoms. *Environmental sustainability* asks a different question again: whether current production and consumption can be maintained without degrading the natural resources and ecosystems that future generations will need. Sustainable development combines these: development that meets present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own.

The relationships between the three are not automatic. Growth need not produce development — output can rise while the gains accrue to a narrow elite, leaving health and education unchanged. And growth is frequently in tension with sustainability, because the cheapest way to raise output in the short run is often to burn fossil fuels, clear forests or over-fish, drawing down natural capital. This is the core conflict: the fastest growth path is commonly the least sustainable one, imposing negative externalities (pollution, climate change) whose cost falls on future generations.

*[Diagram (where appropriate): a negative production externality diagram, with MPC below MSC and market output Qm to the right of the socially efficient Q*, showing the welfare loss from pollution-intensive growth — the gap between private and social cost that unsustainable growth ignores.]*

However, the relationship can also be positive. Growth driven by clean technology and efficient resource use can raise output while remaining sustainable, and rising development — better education, stronger institutions — tends to increase both the willingness and the ability of a society to protect its environment. Sustainable development is therefore the attempt to align all three: to secure growth and development in a form that can be maintained indefinitely.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9–10:** The demands of the question are addressed; relevant economic theory is *fully explained*; relevant economic terms are used appropriately *throughout*; where appropriate, a relevant diagram is included and *fully explained*.
- **7–8:** The demands of the question are addressed; relevant economic theory is *explained*; relevant economic terms are used appropriately for the most part.
- **5–6:** The demands of the question are *partially* addressed; relevant economic theory is *partly explained*; *some* relevant economic terms are used appropriately.
- **3–4:** There is *little* understanding of the specific demands of the question; relevant economic theory is *described*; *some* relevant economic terms are used.
- **1–2:** The answer is *not relevant* to the question.

**Why this response reaches the top band (9–10):**
- ✅ Addresses all three demanded concepts and, crucially, the RELATIONSHIP between them — both the tensions and the reinforcement — not just three separate definitions.
- ✅ Theory fully explained: each concept is defined precisely and then linked, using the negative-externality mechanism to explain WHY growth and sustainability can conflict.
- ✅ Terms used appropriately throughout (real GDP, living standards, natural capital, negative externalities, inter-generational equity).
- ✅ Diagram included where appropriate AND fully explained: the externality diagram is tied back to the argument rather than left unlabelled.
- A response that merely DEFINED the three terms with no explanation of how they relate would sit in the 3–4 band ('theory described'); one that explained the links only partially would sit at 5–6. The move to the top band is driven by fully explaining the relationship and integrating the diagram — exactly what our marking engine rewards.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button below does. Write your own answer to this 10-mark question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that lifts you up the bands here: define growth, development and sustainability separately, then EXPLAIN how they relate, and explain any diagram rather than just drawing it.
Key concepts in this lesson

This lesson develops three of the course's key concepts. **Sustainability** is the whole focus — whether improvements in living standards can be maintained for future generations. **Economic well-being** reminds us that the goal is not output for its own sake but the welfare of people, present and future, which is why development and the environment matter alongside GDP. **Intervention** runs through the policy section: because the environment is a common access resource, markets under-price the damage they do, and governments must step in with taxes, permits, regulation and international agreements. Linking a key concept to real-world material is specifically rewarded in the internal assessment, so keep these three in view. This content is common to SL and HL at this level.
Where this leads

Sustainability ties together threads from across the course: the market-failure and externalities theory of the microeconomics units, the growth-versus-development debates of the development chapters, and the policy tools of macroeconomics. Whenever a later question asks whether a government should subsidise renewables, tax emissions or sign a climate treaty, the underlying question is the one from this lesson: can we raise living standards today without borrowing them from the future?

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: explain the relationship between growth, development and environmental sustainability
